Land Rover Range Rover Velar P380 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Velar P380 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Velar P380 (AJ126) engine typically costs £5,500–£8,500 supply only. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover Velar P380 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ126 supercharged V6 delivers fantastic performance and a great soundtrack. However, it has known weak points: plastic coolant pipes that can fail catastrophically, timing chain tensioner wear, and water pump/thermostat failures. Properly maintained, it can be a reliable engine; neglected, it becomes very expensive.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Velar P380?

acceptedAnswer.text: Plastic coolant pipe and crossover pipe failure (the most critical issue), timing chain tensioner wear, water pump and thermostat housing failure, and supercharger coupler wear. All are manageable with preventative maintenance and early detection.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Velar P380 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a well-maintained Velar (values £25,000–£45,000+), a £6,000–£8,000 reconditioned engine is 15–25% of the vehicle's value — worth it. The Land Rover scrap threshold of 60% means replacement is almost always viable on a Velar, given the model's strong residual values.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Velar P380 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code AJ126 is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The P380 badge was offered on the Velar from launch in 2017 until the 2020 model year update. If your Velar is a 2017–2020 petrol V6, it is the AJ126. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm for free.

mainEntity.name: Should I replace the coolant pipes proactively on my Velar P380?

acceptedAnswer.text: Many specialists recommend proactive replacement of the plastic coolant pipes at around 60,000 miles. The front crossover pipes and rear heater pipe are known failure points, and failure can be catastrophic. JLR has released updated designs, and aftermarket aluminium pipes are available. If your Velar hasn't had this work done, consider it essential preventative maintenance.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover Velar P380 Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Most owners — best cost/warranty balance for this supercharged V6

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the Velar L560 P380 AJ126 era (2017–2020). This engine was replaced by the P400's straight-six in 2020, so no newer-generation variance applies.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Velar P380 engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The supercharger and V6 layout add complexity. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

problems.title: Plastic Coolant Pipe & Crossover Pipe Failure (AJ126)
problems.symptoms: Sudden coolant loss, steam from engine bay, overheating warning, sweet smell, low coolant warning with no visible external leak, coolant pooling under the supercharger.
problems.repairCost: £500–£1,500 (replacement with updated pipes; supercharger removal often required)
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if coolant loss is sudden, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: The AJ126's plastic coolant pipes are the single biggest reliability concern. The front crossover pipes and rear heater pipe are known to crack, often catastrophically. If failure occurs at speed, coolant can drain rapidly, leading to severe overheating and potential engine damage. Some owners recommend proactive replacement of these pipes around 60,000 miles. JLR has released updated designs and aftermarket aluminium replacements are available. If your P380 hasn't had this work done, consider it essential preventative maintenance.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (AJ126)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start lasting 1–3 seconds, engine warning light, loss of power, metallic noises from the front of the engine.
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£8,500 (if chain skips and valves meet pistons)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The AJ126 shares the timing chain tensioner design with the larger AJ133 V8. Extended oil service intervals can accelerate wear on the plastic guides and tensioners. A cold-start rattle is the early warning sign — ignore it and you risk piston-valve contact and a full engine replacement. Use the correct fully synthetic oil and consider reducing oil change intervals to 8,000 miles.

problems.title: Water Pump & Thermostat Housing Failure (AJ126)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, low coolant warning, engine temperature fluctuating, sweet smell from engine bay.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant loss or overheating occurs

problems.recommendation: The AJ126's water pump and thermostat housing are known weak points. Both are plastic components subject to heat cycling and can develop leaks. JLR has issued TSBs for these issues. If you notice coolant loss or temperature fluctuations, have the cooling system pressure-tested immediately.

problems.title: Supercharger Coupler Wear (AJ126)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or ticking noise from the supercharger at idle, reducing as RPM increases, occasional loss of supercharger efficiency.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can lead to supercharger damage

problems.recommendation: The AJ126's supercharger uses a rubber isolator coupler that can wear over time, causing a rattling noise at idle. While not catastrophic, it can lead to supercharger damage if ignored. Replacement involves removing the supercharger and replacing the coupler — a labour-intensive but manageable repair.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Velar P380 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Proactive replacement with updated/aluminium pipes prevents catastrophic failure
Preventative — far cheaper than engine replacement
Straightforward replacement; catch it before overheating
Coupler replacement is cost-effective; catch it before supercharger damage
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Velar P380? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with oil changes at 8,000–10,000 mile intervals
* Updated coolant pipes fitted (aluminium replacements or JLR's revised design)
* No cold-start rattle — timing chain tensioners should be healthy
* Cooling system in good order (recent water pump, thermostat, no coolant loss)
* Supercharger functioning correctly with no rattling at idle
avoidIf: * No proof of regular oil changes — this supercharged V6 is unforgiving of neglect
* Coolant loss with no diagnosis — plastic coolant pipes may be failing
* Cold-start rattle present — timing chain work is imminent
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — the AJ126 can hide expensive faults

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Velar P380 is one of the most desirable variants of JLR's stylish SUV — the AJ126 supercharged V6 delivers 380PS of effortless power and a glorious soundtrack. However, it demands respect: plastic coolant pipes, timing chain tensioners, and water pump/thermostat failures are the three critical weak points. Buy on service history, budget for preventative coolant pipe replacement, and this is a genuinely special car. Neglect the cooling system, and you'll face eye-watering repair bills.

cta.label: → Compare Velar P380 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Velar P380 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle lasting more than 1–2 seconds (timing chain tensioner warning)
* Service History — Oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles are essential for this supercharged V6
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates potential coolant pipe or water pump issues
* Supercharger — Any rattling at idle? Coupler wear is a known issue
* Smoke Check — Blue smoke = oil burning; black smoke = overfuelling
* Engine Temperature — Any fluctuations? Water pump or thermostat may be failing
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Cooling or timing-related codes are most common
* MOT History — Check for emissions failures (often related to fuelling or catalytic converter issues)
